Purpose: Identifying past events of admixture between populations
Specifics: Fast, handles VCF format. Suited for low-depth sequencing (handles uncertainties on genotypes). Provides a set of summary statistics that are useful to investigate complex admixture events
Issues and warnings: Requires an outgroup sequence. The methods cannot estimate the direction of gene flow.

Purpose: Retracing the whole process of recombination and coalescence along a genome
Specifics: Faster than first version of ARGWeaver.
Issues and warnings: Requires phased haplotypes. Specific input format. No built-in functions to extract information from genealogies.

Purpose: Admixture graph, infers most likely admixture events in a tree
Specifics: Allele-frequency-based maximum-likelihood tree with admixture edges. Works on Pool-Seq data. Useful for visualising the relationships among many populations. For testing specific admixture hypotheses on a small number of populations, the qpAdm/qpGraph framework (ADMIXTOOLS 2, admixr) is now generally preferred because it provides explicit model rejection rather than relative likelihoods.
Issues and warnings: Requires multiple runs to assess the likelihood of each model and the OptM heuristic to choose the number of migration edges. Topology results can be unstable when populations are closely related; results should be cross-checked with f-statistics (qpAdm rotation, qpGraph) before firm conclusions are drawn.

Purpose: Estimating population divergence and migration parameters using a coalescent framework
Specifics: Bayesian + MCMC, handles ancient samples
Issues and warnings: Parameters scaled by mutation rate, no admixture

Purpose: Inferring parameters from an isolation with migration (IM) model
Specifics: Fully bayesian approach, can perform joint estimates of parameters in L-mode and test for nested models. Can estimate phylogenetic relationships and migration rates
Issues and warnings: IM model is the only one available. Discrete admixture cannot be tested. Can only use subsets of whole-genome resequencing data. Recent splits lead to overestimate migration rates

Purpose: Model comparison and parameters estimation
Specifics: Uses both allele frequency spectrum and linkage disequilibrium within blocks of a pre-specified size.
Issues and warnings: Relies on ms syntax. Determining the most informative size for blocks requires performing pilot runs.

Purpose: Inferring change in Ne with time
Specifics: User-friendly. Fast. Suitable for pools or low-depth sequencing.
Issues and warnings: Cannot handle migration or population splits.

Purpose: Model comparison and parameters estimation
Specifics: Performs coalescent simulations, parameter estimation and model testing using a fast likelihood method. Can handle arbitrarily complex scenarios for any type of marker
Issues and warnings: The maximum-likelihood method only uses the allele frequency spectrum. Several runs (20-100) are needed to explore the likelihood space.

Purpose: Model comparison and parameters estimation
Specifics: Run time does not depend on the number of SNPs included, does not require coalescent simulations, handles arbitrarily complex scenarios. Fast estimation of confidence intervals around parameters estimates (Godambe method). Suitable for pools/low-depth sequencing
Issues and warnings: requires some knowledge of Python. Limited to 3 populations. Several runs (20-100) are needed to explore the likelihood space.

Purpose: Model comparison and parameters estimation
Specifics: Based on Python, syntax similar to ∂a∂i. Can handle selection. Can use VCF files as input.
Issues and warnings: Requires some knowledge of Python. Limited to 5 populations. Several runs (20-100) are needed to explore the likelihood space.

Purpose: Model comparison and parameters estimation
Specifics: Can scale to ten populations. Can simulate and read data in the VCF format. Detailed tutorials available
Issues and warnings: Does not handle continuous gene flow

Purpose: Estimating divergence time between populations and testing for topologies. Estimate divergence times and past effective sex-ratio along branches of a populations tree.
Specifics: Fast and user-friendly. R scripts to obtain plots are available. Suitable for pools/low-depth sequencing. The method is conditional on a prior topology provided by the user. It computes DIC for a given topology, allowing to test for the best one.
Issues and warnings: Strong selection on the sex chromosome can produce male-biased sex-ratios. Times are given in diffusion time scale, and can be converted in demographic times using independent estimates of Ne.

Purpose: Model comparison and parameters estimation
Specifics: Based on moments and ∂a∂i. Automates the search for the best set of models explaining a given frequency spectrum.
Issues and warnings: Limited to three populations at the moment.

Purpose: Testing various demographic scenario
Specifics: Uses variation in IBD tracts length to test for various demographic models.
Issues and warnings: IBD must be inferred first with, e.g., BEAGLE. Handles a limited set of demographic scenarios. Modification in the code is required for more complex scenarios

Purpose: Predict observed patterns of Identity by state along a genome by fittingan appropriate, arbitrary complex demographic model
Specifics: Allows bootstrapping and estimating confidence over parameter estimates with ms
Issues and warnings: Specific input format (similar to MSMC or ARGWeaver)

Purpose: Builds species trees using short non-recombining sequences
Specifics: Coalescence-based. Suitable for short loci (e.g. RAD-seq and GBS)
Issues and warnings: More reliable under high incomplete lineage sorting that SVDQuartets and NJst (Chou et al. 2015)

Purpose: Network reconstruction and phylogenetic relationships
Specifics: User friendly. Can be used to track changes in effective population sizes (Bayesian Skyline Plots). Possible to estimate divergence times
Issues and warnings: Slow for large datasets. Requires sequence data that can be produced by , e.g., Stacks for RAD-seq data

Purpose: Divergence time estimation and phylogenetic relationships
Specifics: User-friendly, can be run locally or on a webserver, very detailed tutorials. Fast and accurate.
Issues and warnings: Still no tutorial for analyzing big data (last checked December 2020).

Purpose: Divergence time estimation and phylogenetic relationships
Specifics: Included in PAML. A R program is designed to help choosing relevant priors and interpreting results https://github.com/PuttickMacroevolution/MCMCtreeR
Issues and warnings: Bayesian, sensitive to priors. Requires a resolved phylogeny and an alignment. Slow for large datasets. Not suited for recent divergence and high gene flow.

Purpose: Builds species trees using short non-recombining sequences
Specifics: Available in the R package phybase. Estimates populations/species tree from gene trees
Issues and warnings: Requires splitting part of the genome into non-recombining "loci".

Purpose: Admixture graph, reticulated evolution
Specifics: Uses trees in the NEWICK format as an input to infer topology, migration rates, divergence times. Similar to ABC in spirit, using tree topology as a summary statistics.
Issues and warnings: Cannot handle more than 16 taxa at a time, and requires subsetting larger datasets

Purpose: Phylogenetic relationships
Specifics: Maximum Likelihood inference of phylogenetic relationships. An online version is available
Issues and warnings: Should be used on complex of species or divergent populations with little migration. Can be ran on genomic windows to detect introgression (with e.g. TWISST, Dsuite)

Purpose: Network reconstruction and phylogenetic relationships
Specifics: Maximum Likelihood inference of phylogenetic relationships
Issues and warnings: Should be used on complex of species or divergent populations with little migration

Purpose: Phylogenetic relationships
Specifics: Handles SNP data
Issues and warnings: Remains slow for medium to large datasets (>1,000SNPs)

Purpose: Network reconstruction and phylogenetic relationships
Specifics: Complete pipeline from SNP filtering to tree reconstruction
Issues and warnings: Should be used on complex of species or divergent populations with little migration

Purpose: Phylogenetic relationships
Specifics: Estimates populations/species tree from gene trees
Issues and warnings: Remains slow for large datasets. Requires PAUP*.

Purpose: Phylogenetic relationships
Specifics: Estimates populations/species tree from gene trees
Issues and warnings: Faster than SVDQuartets

Purpose: Divergence time estimation and phylogenetic relationships
Specifics: Outputs a species tree instead of concatenated gene tree. Allows for testing consistency between phylogenetic signals at different loci
Issues and warnings: Slow for large datasets. Requires sequence data. Not suited for situations where gene flow/admixture is important

Purpose: Network reconstruction and phylogenetic relationships
Specifics: User friendly interface, proposes a variety of methods for networks reconstruction
Issues and warnings: Mostly descriptive

Purpose: Testing any arbitrary demographic scenario
Specifics: Works with smaller, more fragmented datasets than PSMC. Handles more complex demographic models than MSMC (including admixture).
Issues and warnings: Requires phased whole genome data and a model to be defined

Purpose: Inferring change in Ne and migration rates with time between two populations
Specifics: MSMC2 is the practical successor of MSMC and scales much better with sample size. MSMC-IM jointly estimates Ne(t) and the migration rate between two populations (cross-coalescence-rate-based). Phased data and per-base callability masks are required. ARG-based methods (Relate, SINGER, Threads) cover a similar niche with different tradeoffs.
Issues and warnings: Limited to the study of 8 diploid individuals from 2 populations at once. Requires whole genome phased data and masking regions with insufficient sequencing depth

Purpose: Inferring change in effective population sizes (Ne) with time using a single diploid genome
Specifics: Allows to track population size changes in time without a priori.
Issues and warnings: Limited to one diploid individual and one panmictic population — recent demography is poorly resolved. For multiple individuals use MSMC2 / SMC++; for the recent past (<200 generations) use HapNe / GONE2 / IBDNe. PSMC remains useful as a sanity check on a single high-coverage genome.

Purpose: Inferring change in Ne with time and splitting time between two populations
Specifics: Can analyse hundreds of individuals at a time and does not require phasing - the strongest practical advantage over MSMC2. Estimates Ne(t) with explicit missing-data handling.
Issues and warnings: Recent-time resolution is limited (compensate with HapNe / GONE2 / IBDNe). Mask regions of low callability as in MSMC. The ancestral allele is assumed to be the reference allele by default. Assumes a clean split for two-population divergence; gene-flow inference is approximate.

Purpose: Chromosome painting, clustering and branching between populations
Specifics: Retrieves the most likely coalescence pattern between several taxa along the genome. Can be seen as an extension of the ABBA/BABA test
Issues and warnings: Needs a priori grouping of individuals into taxa. Requires at least 4 taxa. Impractical for more than 6 taxa. Windows size must include enough SNPs to retrieve the correct topology but at the risk that regions with different histories are included

Purpose: Building a population covariance matrix across population allele frequencies, similar to TREEMIX
Specifics: Can handle pooled data
Issues and warnings: Matrices are mostly designed to provide a neutral model for assessing selection, but can be used to infer population structure

Purpose: Identify and date admixture from unsampled (ghost) populations using inferred genealogies
Specifics: EM-based local-ancestry inference on Relate-inferred tree sequences. Detects ghost-population contributions even when no proxy reference exists, by exploiting coalescence-rate signatures along the genome. Returns per-window local ancestry, inferred number of ghost source clusters, and admixture dates via LD-curve fitting (single-pulse, two-pulse, or continuous models). Complementary to cobraa: cobraa works from a single high-coverage diploid; GhostBuster requires multiple samples but localises and dates the admixture along the genome.
Issues and warnings: Currently only supports Relate-inferred trees as input. EM convergence benefits from multiple restarts (default 20). Choice of `--num_clusters` matters — try several. Phasing errors propagate from the Relate step. Computationally heavy at biobank scale; SLURM array workflow is documented.

Purpose: Detect ancient population structure (split-and-rejoin / ghost-lineage ancestry) from a single diploid genome
Specifics: Coalescent HMM extending PSMC to a structured-ancestry model with an ancient split between two populations and a later admixture event. Estimates split time, admixture time, mixing proportions and ancestral Ne(t). Applied to humans it identifies a ~80:20 admixture ~300 ka after a ~1.5 Ma split. Generalises to any species with a high-coverage diploid genome.
Issues and warnings: Single diploid input - per-individual; requires high coverage and a recombination map. The split-and-rejoin model is one specific structured demography; not all structured histories will fit it. Convergence and identifiability depend on the chosen number of time intervals.

Purpose: Detect IBD segments and infer pairwise coalescence times at biobank scale
Specifics: ASMC: ascertained-SMC HMM that estimates pairwise TMRCA along the genome from phased data. FastSMC adds a hashing-based candidate identification step (improved GERMLINE) so the HMM only verifies likely IBD segments — scales to hundreds of thousands of phased individuals with TMRCA estimates per segment. Used on 487k UK Biobank samples to map fine-scale UK structure.
Issues and warnings: Phased data and a recombination map required. Strong selection biases the inferred TMRCA distribution (see Plasmodium benchmarks). For Ne(t) inference, combine with HapNe-IBD or IBDNe.
Reference: (Palamara et al., 2018; Nait Saada et al., 2020)

Purpose: Test specific admixture models with explicit rejection
Specifics: f-statistic-based test that asks whether a target population can be modelled as a mixture of N source populations, given a set of 'right' (outgroup) populations. The 'rotation' strategy (Harney et al. 2021) systematically rotates candidate sources and references to find all plausible models. Now the de facto admixture inference framework, complementing the visualisation provided by TreeMix. Available via classical ADMIXTOOLS, ADMIXTOOLS 2 (Maier 2023), and admixr.
Issues and warnings: Highly sensitive to the choice of right populations. Sets that are too closely related to the sources lose power; sets that are too distant can fail to reject incorrect models. Always run rotation rather than a single hand-picked set, and report all plausible models, not just the lowest-p one.
Reference: (Haak et al., 2015; Harney et al., 2021; Maier et al., 2023)

Purpose: Estimate Ne(t) over the past ~200 generations from LD, with explicit handling of population structure and data-quality issues
Specifics: Successor to GONE that corrects the strong over-estimation of recent Ne reported under structure / admixture / suboptimal genotyping. Now the recommended LD-based recent-Ne tool.
Issues and warnings: Still recombination-map dependent; performance under selfing or strong inbreeding requires verification.

Purpose: Estimate contemporary Ne with structure-aware corrections
Specifics: Updated currentNe with neural-network corrections for population structure and genotyping quality, paired with GONE2 in the same Santiago 2025 release.
Issues and warnings: Best for contemporary Ne; structure correction is approximate. Newer than the original currentNe — supersedes it.

Purpose: Bayesian inference of hybrid index, ancestry proportions, geographic clines and hierarchical genomic clines from hybrid-zone data
Specifics: R package, successor of bgc. Uses Hamiltonian Monte Carlo and accepts genotype likelihoods (works with low-coverage data). Quantifies among-locus variation in introgression — key for testing reproductive-isolation hypotheses.
Issues and warnings: Computationally heavier than classical cline fitting; requires care in defining parental reference samples.

Purpose: Quantify variation in coupling along the genome (aggregate vs. locus-specific selection effects during speciation)
Specifics: Window-based estimator of Barton's coupling coefficient applied to whole-genome data from a hybrid zone; tests predictions of coupling theory (relations to recombination, linkage, selection).
Issues and warnings: Designed for hybrid-zone data; requires sufficient sampling across the cline. Benchmarked on rattlesnakes — generalisation to other systems still being explored.

Purpose: Scalable inference of tree sequences from large-scale variant data, plus dating of nodes
Specifics: Heuristic threading-based inference scaling to millions of samples; tsdate adds variational dating. Underlies the Wohns et al. unified human genealogy.
Issues and warnings: Topology more reliable than dates; assumes infinite sites; phasing/genotyping errors propagate. Background selection and demography bias node ages.

Purpose: Biobank-scale ARG inference, primarily for genotyping arrays
Specifics: Threading-based; designed to enable genealogy-based GWAS on UK Biobank-like cohorts.
Issues and warnings: Requires a precomputed Ne(t) prior to date branches; single-point estimate, no posterior.

Purpose: Bayesian posterior sampling of ARGs with topology + branch-length uncertainty
Specifics: MCMC over the SMC with sub-graph pruning/regrafting; orders of magnitude faster than ARGweaver while retaining its functionality. Recommended when uncertainty quantification matters.
Issues and warnings: Currently scales to hundreds of WGS, not biobanks; computationally heavier than heuristic methods.

Purpose: Very fast ARG inference at biobank scale (sequencing + genotyping arrays)
Specifics: Composite-HMM threading; the most scalable current ARG inference method.
Issues and warnings: Single-point estimate without posterior; phasing-error sensitivity quantified in recent benchmarks.

Purpose: Likelihood inference of complex demographic models (split, admixture, asymmetric migration, stepping-stone) from inferred or simulated ARGs
Specifics: Composite likelihood on pairwise coalescence times under a continuous-time Markov process; designed for stepping-stone and continuous-migration models that are awkward in SFS-based frameworks.
Issues and warnings: Inherits errors from upstream ARG inference; requires a prespecified model topology.

Purpose: Felsenstein-style likelihood inference of demographic models directly from inferred genealogies
Specifics: Computes likelihoods on tree-sequence representations of ARGs.
Issues and warnings: Sensitive to ARG quality; computational cost grows with model complexity.

Purpose: Bayesian sampling of ARGs
Specifics: Alternative Bayesian sampler comparable to ARGweaver/SINGER for moderate sample sizes.
Issues and warnings: Limited scalability; benchmarking overlap with SINGER.

Purpose: Joint estimation of Ne(t), allele ages, and detection of selection from inferred genealogies
Specifics: Already widely used; the Speidel et al. selection extension scans the genealogy for excess descendants under positive selection.
Issues and warnings: Requires phased data and recombination map; phasing errors and demographic mis-specification can confound selection scans.

Purpose: Estimate Ne(t) over the past ~50 generations from the IBD segment length distribution
Specifics: Non-parametric likelihood. Standard for recent Ne in human cohorts.
Issues and warnings: Requires accurate phasing and dense markers; spurious oscillations in the very recent past, mitigated by HapNe-IBD.

Purpose: Estimate Ne(t) over the past ~2000 years from LD or IBD; works on low-coverage and aDNA
Specifics: Regularised non-parametric inference; HapNe-LD does not require phasing and works on SNP arrays. Handles heterogeneous sampling times.
Issues and warnings: Recombination-map dependence; structure / cryptic admixture inflate apparent recent Ne. HapNe-LD has a built-in admixture-LD test.

Purpose: Estimate Ne over the past ~200 generations from LD across genetic distances
Specifics: Linear regression on LD vs. recombination distance; widely used in conservation and non-model species.
Issues and warnings: Sensitive to genetic-map quality; inflated under structure; can over-fit fluctuations. Recent benchmarks (Delord et al. 2025) report systematic overestimation.

Purpose: Estimate current/recent Ne from a single sample
Specifics: LD-based with neural-network correction for structure and data quality; lower computational cost than GONE; tolerates small samples.
Issues and warnings: Best for contemporary Ne; structure correction is approximate.

Purpose: Estimate contemporary Ne from LD, heterozygote excess, molecular coancestry or temporal samples
Specifics: Implements multiple single-sample and two-sample estimators; the standard reference in conservation genetics.
Issues and warnings: Single-point Ne; biased under structure / overlapping generations; limited to relatively small panels.

Purpose: Date founder bottlenecks from allele-sharing decay
Specifics: Decay-of-allele-sharing model; complements DATES for bottleneck rather than admixture timing.
Issues and warnings: Targets a single founder event; needs sufficient post-founding drift.

Purpose: Date admixture events from ancestry-covariance decay
Specifics: Two-population LD/ancestry decay; widely used in archaeogenetics.
Issues and warnings: Single-pulse model; needs differentiated reference panels.

Purpose: Date admixture from weighted LD decay; MALDER allows multiple pulses
Specifics: Uses LD between SNPs differentiated between reference populations; standard pre-DATES approach.
Issues and warnings: Sensitive to reference choice; multiple pulses are partially confounded.

Purpose: Joint inference of demography from SFS and two-locus LD statistics
Specifics: Adds LD information to moments to improve recent-Ne resolution; integrates with demes and stdpopsim.
Issues and warnings: Computationally heavier than SFS alone; LD pre-computation step required.

Purpose: Command-line interface and reproducible pipelines for dadi inference
Specifics: Lowers the barrier for running dadi at scale; integrates with demes models.
Issues and warnings: Same SFS-based assumptions as dadi (independent sites etc.).

Purpose: Standard YAML format for demographic models, consumed by msprime / moments / dadi / fwdpy11
Specifics: Not a simulator itself, but the de facto interchange format; demesdraw provides standard schematics.
Issues and warnings: Expressivity is limited to discrete-deme models with piecewise-constant rates.

Purpose: Estimate Ne(t) from a single (PSMC') or several (MSMC2) phased diploids; MSMC-IM extends to migration
Specifics: Successors of PSMC and MSMC; MSMC2 has improved scaling for tens of haplotypes; MSMC-IM jointly infers Ne and migration.
Issues and warnings: Phasing required for MSMC2; recent-time resolution is poor (compensate with HapNe / GONE).

Purpose: Joint inference of Ne(t), recombination-rate landscape and self-fertilisation rates
Specifics: Extensions of PSMC handling selfing, polyploidy and explicit heterogeneity in recombination.
Issues and warnings: Slow on whole genomes; demographic mis-specification biases the recombination map.

Purpose: Particle-filter-based SMC inference of population size and migration
Specifics: Continuous-time Markov-jump particle filter; handles multi-population history.
Issues and warnings: Computationally heavy; less mainstream than MSMC2.

Purpose: Estimate Ne(t) via ABC on summary statistics, including recent past
Specifics: Combines AFS- and LD-based summaries to capture both recent and ancient changes.
Issues and warnings: Sensitive to summary-statistic choice; requires extensive simulation.

Purpose: Likelihood inference of two-population history from blockwise SFS
Specifics: Useful when SFS-based methods conflate joint and marginal information.
Issues and warnings: Mostly tested on humans / Drosophila; recombination-rate variation can bias.

Purpose: Non-parametric Ne(t) from the unfolded or folded SFS
Specifics: Stairway 2 adds bootstrap-based uncertainty; epos provides additional smoothing options.
Issues and warnings: Limited resolution at recent timescales; sensitive to genotype-calling biases.

Purpose: Manipulate and analyse tree sequences output by msprime, SLiM, tsinfer, Relate (via converters)
Specifics: Provides the unified data structure underlying most modern ARG-based analyses; not a method per se but a critical dependency.
Issues and warnings: Requires familiarity with the tree-sequence model.
